source.title: Reverse replay of behavioural sequences in hippocampal place cells during the awake state

confidence_rationale: Foundational empirical demonstration that awake-quiescent hippocampal replay carries content in a temporally-reversed order relative to waking experience. This is the single cleanest piece of evidence that the substrate has a structural property (reverse temporal order) which downstream regions could in principle use as a discriminative signal to distinguish replay-driven inputs from waking inputs -- exactly the substrate-level marker MECH-094's hypothesis-tag would need. Source quality very high (Nature, Wilson lab). Mapping fidelity moderate-to-high (the discriminative-signature exists; whether downstream regions actually USE it as a write-gate is not demonstrated here).
failure_signatures: Foster & Wilson demonstrate the discriminable signature exists but do not test whether any downstream region gates writes on the basis of replay vs waking temporal direction.

The reverse-replay phenomenon is observed during awake quiescent rest immediately after experience, not during arbitrary simulation -- the timing constraint may be too tight for REE's general simulation-mode tag.

mapping.source_claim_statement: During pauses in active behaviour on a linear track, hippocampal place-cell sequences replay the just-experienced trajectory in temporally REVERSED order (last-visited place fires first). Replay events are repeated, are not random, and are temporally compressed relative to waking experience. The reverse-temporal-order signature is structurally distinct from any sequence that would have been emitted during waking active running.
mapping.ree_translation: REE's MECH-094 hypothesis-tag posits that simulation/replay-driven trajectories carry a discriminable marker that downstream rule-write machinery can read to suppress the post-commit write channel. Foster & Wilson demonstrate that one such marker exists at the substrate level: the temporal direction of place-cell firing during awake quiescent replay is structurally inverted relative to waking experience, and is therefore in-principle available as a discriminable signal. This is the most concrete biological precondition for MECH-319's proposed mechanism. The 2006 paper does not show that downstream regions actually exploit this signal as a write-gate, but it establishes that the signal is there to be exploited.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Two limits. (1) Reverse-replay specifically is one of several SWR-time signatures (forward replay, preplay, mixed orderings); the FULL set of replay-time signatures and whether they cluster cleanly into a 'simulation mode vs not' binary is unsettled. REE's categorical-tag framing may be cleaner than the empirical phenomenology supports. (2) The reverse-replay phenomenon was characterised on a behaviourally-driven 1D track; whether the same temporal-direction signature obtains for non-spatial REE-relevant tasks (object choice, harm avoidance) is an extrapolation.
mapping.source_context: Single-unit recording in 4 rats running on linear tracks for food reward. Awake-quiescent replay events detected in the seconds-to-minutes after each lap. Sequences decoded by population firing reconstruction.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality empirical paper. The structural marker REE needs (a discriminable replay-vs-waking signature) is demonstrated; the functional use of that marker (gating write rules) is REE's contribution to test.
